stories.title: A Devotion to Saving Graves and History
stories.summary: Jason Bono, 25, turns headstone cleaning into a small business and history project, restoring weathered graves in East Hampton. Using nontoxic solutions, he respectfully preserves the stories of those who built the town, ensuring their legacy isn't forgotten.
stories.extended_summary: For years, names on weatherworn headstones faded beneath layers of dirt and lichen—until Jason Bono began cleaning them one at a time. The 25-year-old turned his Boy Scout passion into Hampton Headstone Care, using specially made nontoxic solutions to restore graves without harming plant or animal life. His goal is to preserve local history, especially as families move away and ancestors' headstones go uncared for. Even abandoned cemeteries get attention, with the town stepping in. Bono’s work ensures that the history of those who built East Hampton remains visible for generations.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Starts Library for Forgotten Languages in Her Basement

stories.title: A student’s expedition to the Antarctic
stories.summary: Eric Gee went from Honolulu to Antarctica on a mission that changed him inside. The student helped research, broadcasted to 2 million kids, and came back with a deeper purpose: protecting our only Earth. His journey proves one trip can shift your whole perspective.
stories.extended_summary: Nothing prepares you for stepping onto Antarctic ice—Eric Gee can tell you that. The University of Hawaii student joined an expedition with explorer Robert Swan, helping research and teaching 2 million students live from the frozen continent. He saw glaciers melting and felt the weight of climate change firsthand. His mom always told him to leave things better than he found it; now he's passing that lesson on to a generation.
stories.fake_headline: Local Teen Wins International Science Fair With Project on Cleaning Ocean Plastics

stories.title: After a Road Accident Left Him in a Wheelchair, This 14 YO Started Mapping Accessible Places for Others
stories.summary: After a road accident left him in a wheelchair at 13, Pratik Shingare realized how inaccessible cities are. He started 'StairlessJourney,' mapping over 50 accessible spaces across India—so no one else feels shut out. From personal struggle to community lifeline.
stories.extended_summary: Pratik Shingare was 13 when a road accident put him in a wheelchair and his school refused to take him back. Suddenly, steps and narrow doorways became impossible barriers. Instead of giving up, he started mapping places that actually welcome wheelchair users. 'StairlessJourney' has now cataloged over 50 accessible spots across India. He's building a future app to help others find their way in a world that wasn't built for them.
stories.headline_split: After a Road Accident Left Him in a Wheelchair, This 14 YO Started Mapping
stories.fake_headline: Three Sisters Launch Free Tutoring Program for Underprivileged Kids in Rural India

stories.title: Alone on graduation day, Minnesota student finds a global family through viral TikTok
stories.summary: Miguell Schmerbeck walked the graduation stage alone—no mom, no dad, no one to cheer him. His honest TikTok about it went viral, raising $23K and inviting him to a global grad party. Millions showed up for someone who thought he had nobody.
stories.extended_summary: Miguell Schmerbeck posted himself crying on graduation day, text reading: 'Everybody had someone waiting. I had nobody.' His mother died when he was 13; his father was absent. Then 20 million views and 200,000 comments flooded in. Strangers Venmo'd him, told him they were proud, and offered a place at their own parties. A GoFundMe raised over $23K. He went from feeling invisible to finding a global family in the comments.

stories.title: An Eagle Scout Project that Soars – “Go Home Kits” for Adopted Pets
stories.summary: Eagle Scout Ethan Moskowitz delivers 40 'Go Home Kits' to Last Hope animal shelter. Each kit includes a bed, food, toys, and care instructions for newly adopted pets, inspired by his own rescued Beagle Chip.
stories.extended_summary: When Ethan Moskowitz adopted his Beagle Chip from Last Hope seven years ago, he never imagined it would inspire his Eagle Scout project. As a senior at Wantagh High School, Ethan organized a team to create 40 'Go Home Kits' for newly adopted cats and dogs. Each cardboard carton contains a bed, blanket, Science Diet food, toys, bowls, and a care guide. The project took three months of planning and fundraising. Now, each kit gives a new pet family everything they need to start their lives together — all because of a boy and his rescue dog.

stories.title: Couple fulfills yearslong dream by turning Fishtown rowhome garage into a music venue
stories.summary: Joe Lockwood and Morgan Walbridge turned their Fishtown rowhome garage into Nostalgia Fishtown—a thrift store, music venue, and rehearsal space. After years of house hunting and a city bust, they're now booking five shows a week through November.
stories.extended_summary: On a quiet side street in Fishtown, a rowhome garage now doubles as a thrumming music venue. Joe Lockwood and Morgan Walbridge spent years chasing the underground gig culture they loved at art school—and after a permit scare that nearly ended it all, Nostalgia Fishtown is open. Passersby can already buy T-shirts and peek at the space; the first concert is June 28.

stories.title: From Losses to ₹12 Lakh/Month | The Benne Story
stories.summary: Ganesh and Sapna Sathe started with an old cart and their mom's benne dosa recipe—losing ₹200 every day. Then social media found them, and now they earn ₹12 lakh a month, selling out in an hour. Proof that persistence (and a killer recipe) can flip the script.
stories.extended_summary: The Benne stall in Thane used to bleed money—₹200 losses daily from a pushcart. Siblings Ganesh and Sapna kept tweaking the batter and the setup, refusing to quit. Then a customer's Instagram post made their crispy benne dosas go viral. Now people line up before opening, and everything's gone in 60 minutes. Revenue hit ₹12 lakh last month. No investor, no connections—just a mother's recipe and stubborn hope.

stories.title: How Two Students Built an $80K Month Lake Service
stories.summary: At 15 and 16, Joe and Declan started clearing lake weeds with a garden rake and a laundry basket. Fast forward: their seasonal lake service pulls in $80K a month, books months ahead, and proves you don't need money to start something huge—just a problem people will pay to fix.
stories.extended_summary: Two teenagers with a rake and a wet laundry basket didn't look like entrepreneurs—but homeowners couldn't stand the weeds. Joe Weinberger and Declan started as dock installers, then a client asked them to clear their shoreline. Word spread door-to-door. Two years as a side gig, then full-time. Now their lake and pond service rakes in nearly $80,000 in peak months, with zero paid ads. They kept it simple: solve one painful problem, then scale.

stories.title: How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China
stories.summary: A 24-year-old engineering student fought a highway project to protect the critically endangered spoon-billed sandpiper—a bird he'd never seen. He and thousands of advocates flooded officials with emails, and China's government actually halted the plans. A rare win for a near-extinct species.
stories.extended_summary: Li Jiahe had never visited the mudflats or seen the spoon-billed sandpiper—but when a planned highway threatened the wintering habitat of just 443 remaining birds, he started emailing. For weeks, he and thousands of strangers sent letters to Chinese authorities and international groups. On May 25, central environmental authorities stopped the project. The province agreed to reconsider. One student, a species on the brink, and a highway put on hold.
stories.source_url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018593/How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China

stories.title: Nobody Taught Them to Adult — So They’re Teaching Each Other
stories.summary: Thousands of teens age out of India's childcare institutions every year with no support. So two care leavers built CLiC—a platform run by former care kids, for current ones. They're teaching each other how to adult when nobody else will.
stories.extended_summary: Girish Mehta had one month to figure out life after leaving his childcare home at 18. Anisha Sharma was 'mid-course, mid-dream, and on my own.' They knew the system wasn't working—30,000 teens age out yearly in India, often with no aftercare. So they created Careleavers Inner Circle (CLiC), a tech startup led by care leavers that offers mentorship, resources, and community. It's young people saving their own futures.

stories.title: On The House founder Remy Tucker’s desire to tackle period poverty didn’t start in marketing… it started in a hospital
stories.summary: Remy Tucker saw period poverty as a midwife and later as a marketer. She realized the problem wasn't just poverty—it was access. So she created ON THE HOUSE, raising $1.7M to make period products free where people actually need them: bathrooms.
stories.extended_summary: As a student midwife, Remy Tucker watched women scramble for period products in hospitals—a constant, invisible friction. Then she saw brands spend billions on digital ads that women hated. The insight: toilet paper is free in bathrooms, so why not pads and tampons? After two years of pitching and failing, she raised $1.7M to launch ON THE HOUSE, putting free period products in high-traffic bathrooms. The goal: make access a basic amenity, not a luxury.

stories.title: Shell Game: The Sisters Behind Hawaiʻi’s Hottest Oyster Company
stories.summary: Sisters Allie and Julie turned a love for oysters into Hawaiʻi's hottest catering company—and they launched a week before the pandemic hit. Now they shuck at the Super Bowl and serve across four islands. Dream + execution = a shell of a success story.
stories.extended_summary: Allie Chu, a former Miss Hawaiʻi and opera singer, saw a gap: no oyster catering in the Islands. With her husband, she started Oysters Hawaiʻi days before COVID shut everything down. Enter sister Julie—a teacher turned 'executioner' who turned big ideas into spreadsheets. After the pause, one wedding booking reignited everything. Now they shuck across Hawaiʻi, Las Vegas, and California—even at Super Bowl LVIII. Two sisters, one vision, and a lot of oysters.

stories.title: The Only Miami-Dade Nonprofit Catching NICU Families Before They Fall | Miami Autism Dad
stories.summary: Elizabeth Simonton saw NICU families falling through the cracks after her own son's stay. So she built ICU baby—South Florida's only nonprofit offering parent mentors, meals, transport, and bereavement support in three languages. No family should sit alone by a baby's bed.
stories.extended_summary: Sitting next to her son's NICU bed in 2014, Elizabeth Simonton saw what she couldn't unsee: families struggling alone, without gas money, without meals, without someone who understood. She co-founded ICU baby, now serving Miami-Dade with parent mentors, NICU Packs, catered meals, transportation stipends, and bereavement support in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole. The homepage says it three times: babies should not be alone.
stories.headline_split: The Only Miami-Dade Nonprofit Catching NICU Families

stories.title: 3 Lakh Trees & Counting: Meet the Urban Green Expert Bringing Forests Back to India's Most Crowded Cities
stories.summary: Subhajit Mukherjee is turning Mumbai's tiniest unused corners into dense pocket forests. With just 100 square feet and native saplings, anyone can create a thriving mini-ecosystem. His Mission Green Mumbai proves that even the most crowded cities can bloom again.
stories.extended_summary: At a government hospital in Malad and along a navy school's walls, you'll find unexpected thickets alive with birdsong and butterflies. These are pocket forests, planted by Subhajit Mukherjee in as little as 100 square feet. Through Mission Green Mumbai, he's shown that India's cities aren't too crowded for forests—they just need to think small. He's planted over 3 lakh trees so far, and he says anyone with a WhatsApp group and a tiny patch of land can do the same.

stories.title: Accessible Tools and Printing with Milwaukee Tool Library and Shaky Hands
stories.summary: The Milwaukee Tool Library lets community members borrow tools and equipment for free, from hand drills to wet saws. Founder Evan Maruszewski believes sharing what you have with a neighbor is a tangible way to improve the world.
stories.extended_summary: At the Milwaukee Tool Library, neighbors can borrow tools free of charge—from power drills to compressors—thanks to founder Evan Maruszewski's belief that access matters more than market ownership. The library also houses Shaky Hands, a printing cooperative. Maruszewski explains that a typical power drill sees only 12 minutes of use in its lifetime; collective ownership makes full use of its utility. The space aims to empower the community by removing barriers to tools and equipment for home and commercial projects.

stories.title: Can Your Plants Scream In Pain?
stories.summary: Swedish startup Sonicflora is listening to plants' ultrasonic sounds to detect stress before visible signs appear. By training AI on sounds from dehydrated or diseased plants, they aim to help farmers intervene early. Your next houseplant might just tell you it's thirsty.
stories.extended_summary: Plants scream—just not in a way we can hear. Sonicflora has placed healthy plants in soundproof chambers, introduced stressors like drought, and recorded the ultrasonic 'melodies' they emit. When a plant is dehydrated, tiny air bubbles burst in its tissues, creating airborne signals. The company's AI learns to recognize these distress calls, potentially allowing growers to water or treat crops before any visible damage. So far, they're focusing on indoor tomatoes, but the tech could one day save your cactus from an early grave.
stories.fake_headline: Robot Dog Helps Autistic Children Learn Social Skills in School

stories.title: Eat This Now: Rasta pasta at Wah Gwan
stories.summary: Eighth-grader Lataya Reid lands her first byline in a major publication with a review of Wah Gwan's Rasta Pasta. She describes the vibrant restaurant, the reggae vibes, and a dish so good her friend promised it would 'change your life.' Spicy, creamy, and unforgettable.
stories.extended_summary: "You have to try this, it will change your life, fam." That's what Lataya Reid's friend told her before entering Wah Gwan, a Jamaican spot in Kansas City with a tropical mural and old-school reggae records. Inside, the smell of grilled chicken fills the air, and the Rasta Pasta is the star—a creamy, spicy dish that lives up to the hype. Lataya, an 8th grader, wrote this review as part of a class project, and The Pitch chose it as one of the top three submissions to publish. Her first byline, and she's already making us hungry.
stories.fake_headline: New Library App Lets You Borrow E-Books Instantly Using Just Your Phone

stories.title: In Search of Seclusion on the Olympic Peninsula
stories.summary: Adam and Sara Simon bought a property on the Olympic Peninsula that was slated for a 50-cabin resort. Instead, they created Aliya Preserve—a rustic campground with just five sites, two cabins, and two tiny wooden pods. They kept it wild, adding an outdoor shower with a Pacific view and removing invasive species to let nature thrive.
stories.extended_summary: An eagle swoops over an estuary as Adam Simon points it out, his train of thought lost to the beauty around him. He's showing off Aliya Preserve, his 25-acre private campground near Kalaloch, Washington. Where a global hospitality giant planned dozens of cabins, Adam and his wife Sara kept things simple: five campsites, two tiny 'AliyaPods' with no plumbing, and an outdoor shower facing the Pacific. They tore out Himalayan blackberries and alder thickets to boost biodiversity, leaving the rest as wild as possible. The result is a place so peaceful that even the owner keeps getting distracted by the view.

stories.title: Northwestern wheelchair basketball pushes for a team
stories.summary: Northwestern University launched a wheelchair basketball team after co-founders Alec Cabacungan and Sam Milgrom turned a one-on-one challenge into inclusive pickup games. The first event drew 15-20 players—way more than the eight they expected—including members of the women's club basketball team.
stories.extended_summary: The sound of basketball shoes was replaced by the hum of wheels at Northwestern's gym. Co-founders hoped for eight players; they got nearly 20, and the energy was electric. The team was born from a 2020 challenge between a student and his adviser—both basketball lovers. Now, players of all abilities compete together, proving that sports can be accessible to everyone.

stories.title: Saeed’s Punk Nite puts on free theme parties with games and nostalgic playlists at Maggie’s Rumble Room
stories.summary: Every second Monday, Saeed Tokhi transforms Maggie's Rumble Room into a punk-themed party with games, nostalgic playlists, and a red fez. From 'Draw Your Own Black Sheep' contests to Tony Hawk's Pro Skater soundtrack nights, it's an interactive, multi-generational celebration of punk attitude. One year in, it's become a Des Moines staple.
stories.extended_summary: At the far end of Maggie's Rumble Room, a sheet hangs with a shag carpet beneath it. That's where Saeed Tokhi—wearing his signature red fez with a lightning bolt 'N'—hosts Saeed's Punk Nite. Each month brings a new theme: a Minor Threat-inspired 'Banned in DC' night with a Black Sheep drawing contest, or a Tony Hawk's Pro Skater night with songs from the game's soundtrack. The bar serves themed drinks, and the crowd spans generations. Tokhi believes 'punk is an attitude,' and after a year of these free parties, Des Moines agrees.
stories.headline_split: Saeed’s Punk Nite puts on free theme parties with games and nostalgic playlists

stories.title: What if Vacant Spaces Could Be Used as Artist Studios?
stories.summary: Evan Snow and Andrew Martineau started Zero Empty Spaces to turn vacant storefronts into affordable artist studios. Since 2019, they've created 34 spaces across five states—each about 96 square feet for under $400 a month, with utilities covered and no commission taken. Artists keep 100% of their sales.
stories.extended_summary: What if every empty storefront could become an artist's studio? That's the question Evan Snow and Andrew Martineau asked—and then answered. Their nonprofit, Zero Empty Spaces, takes vacant commercial spaces and renovates them into affordable studios for artists, ghost kitchens, and co-retailing hubs. Since 2019, they've opened 34 spaces in five states. Each artist gets about 96 square feet for $384 a month, with utilities and communal displays paid for. Artists keep every dime they make. The next space is coming to downtown Minneapolis, proving that empty spaces don't have to stay empty.

stories.title: Where Memphis Melts
stories.summary: The Memphis Ice Cream Festival started as a small Facebook event between two local ice cream makers. It sold out 1,000 tickets and is now returning bigger than ever for Overton Park's 125th birthday. Expect unlimited tastings, food trucks, and family fun—plus, of course, ice cream.
stories.extended_summary: Mary Claire White of Sugar Ghost and Kiamesha Wilson of Kaye's Pints & Scoops met at 'Scoop School' in St. Louis. Both had the same dream: an ice cream festival for Memphis. They created a Facebook event, and it went viral. Last year's inaugural festival sold out at 1,000 tickets, so this year they're partnering with Overton Park Conservancy for a bigger venue and a joint celebration of the park's 125th birthday. The festival returns June 7th with unlimited ice cream tastings, food trucks, music, and family activities. It's the sweetest way to ring in summer.

stories.title: A Husband’s Diagnosis Inspired This New Hampshire Gluten-Free Bakery Built on Love
stories.summary: When Angela's husband was diagnosed with celiac disease, she started baking gluten-free treats in her Belmont kitchen. Her recipes were so good that Gluten Free or Die Bakery was born—a 'homestead kitchen' that has never touched gluten. Since 2021, she's been delivering joy to customers across New Hampshire.
stories.extended_summary: Angela, a baker originally from Chile, started experimenting in her kitchen when her husband was diagnosed with celiac disease and other food sensitivities. She wanted to recreate the foods he missed most—without compromise. Soon, she discovered her own autoimmune condition and went gluten-free herself. What began as family baking evolved into Gluten Free or Die Bakery, operating from her Belmont 'homestead kitchen' that has never contained gluten. Since 2021, she's been selling cookies, muffins, and bread that taste like the real thing. Her secret ingredient? Love.
stories.headline_split: A Husband’s Diagnosis Inspired This New Hampshire

stories.title: AI is taking on antibiotic resistance — here’s how
stories.summary: Researchers at McMaster University have used AI to discover 'enterololin'—a molecule that specifically targets harmful E. coli while leaving good gut bacteria unharmed. The compound was found after screening 10,000 bioactive compounds, and it could lead to precision antibiotics that don't fuel resistance.
stories.extended_summary: Most antibiotics are like sledgehammers—they kill good bacteria along with the bad, especially in the gut. For people with Crohn's disease, that can be devastating. Jonathan Stokes and his team at McMaster University turned to AI to find a smarter weapon. They screened 10,000 compounds and landed on just one molecule: enterololin. It specifically targets harmful E. coli without harming the rest of the microbiome. The discovery could lead to precision antibiotics that treat infections without triggering resistance or destroying gut health. It's a step toward antibiotics that work like scalpels, not sledgehammers.
stories.headline_split: AI is taking on antibiotic resistance —

stories.title: Afrofuturism comes to Garfield through public artwork
stories.summary: Pittsburgh artist Shamir Walters debuts 'Everyday Afrofuturism,' an interactive sculpture in Garfield featuring Octavia Butler, Alice Coltrane, and Herbie Hancock. The kinetic installation invites community engagement and expands ideas about Black creativity and possibility.
stories.extended_summary: Artist Shamir Walters — who calls himself a 'futurist with no Afro' — has unveiled an interactive sculpture in Pittsburgh's Garfield neighborhood. 'Everyday Afrofuturism' features rotating panels of Octavia Butler, Alice Coltrane, and Herbie Hancock, three cultural icons who pushed boundaries of Black creativity. The work grew out of years of Afrofuturism-themed STEAM education with Assemble and BOOM Concepts. Walters, who has Cherokee, Seminole, and Choctaw roots, resists easy categorization, but his art invites everyone to imagine the future.

stories.title: For pregnant mothers in Philadelphia, they can receive a 'Home From Shana'
stories.summary: A Philadelphia nonprofit honors LaShana Gilmore, who died in childbirth, by providing free housing and support for pregnant women experiencing homelessness. Their LaborLiveLove program gives moms a stable home and postpartum care.
stories.extended_summary: LaShana Gilmore was healthy, college-educated—and her concerns still went unheard. She died after giving birth in 2019. Her best friend turned grief into action, founding A Home From Shana. Now, through the LaborLiveLove program, pregnant women facing unstable housing get a safe place to stay, plus support groups and resources. It's a lifeline for mothers who need a home before their babies arrive.

stories.title: High School Sports in Jackson County Get a Marketing Makeover
stories.summary: Jackson County high school sports are getting a marketing makeover. PA announcer Wesley Johnson brings the energy with his 'JaxCo Radio Network' routine, while athletic directors use social media, sponsorships, and game-day entertainment to boost attendance and revenue. The NFHS says high school is an underestimated market.
stories.extended_summary: Wesley Johnson's sequined jacket glistens under the lights as he hypes the crowd at a Jackson County soccer game. "Head to the concession stand for our world-famous JaxCo nachos!" he shouts, and fans laugh and cheer. Johnson is part of a push to bring commercial flair to high school sports. With rising costs, athletic directors are using social media, sponsorships, and entertaining PA announcers to draw bigger crowds and generate revenue. The National Federation of State High School Associations is even offering consulting services, noting that brands are eager to reach high school students. It's a whole new ballgame for high school athletics.

stories.title: In Philadelphia, a New Art Fair Removes Scale From the Equation
stories.summary: Philadelphia's art scene just got a fresh boost with Elsewhere, a new fair that's intentionally small and affordable. Booth fees max out at $2,500 (including lodging), and artwork tops out at $25,000. It's a space for experimentation, proving that big things can happen without big budgets.
stories.extended_summary: Megan Galardi, director of Blah Blah Gallery, created Elsewhere to let Philadelphia thrive on its own terms — not as a New York wannabe. Inspired by curator-led fairs like Arrival Art Fair and Friends Fair, she capped fees and artwork prices to encourage risk-taking over commercial pressure. With booth fees as low as $2,500 and lodging included, galleries can experiment freely. The fair turns Philadelphia into a crucible for new ideas in the art world.

stories.title: Investing in Logan Elementary
stories.summary: Logan Elementary in Philadelphia is transforming its 2.2 acres of gray pavement into a green space with a track, basketball courts, and gardens. The community-driven project is designed by third graders and backed by multiple partners.
stories.extended_summary: When Eli Prosper looks at Logan Elementary's concrete schoolyard, he sees a basketball court, a bike track, and green spaces designed by the third graders themselves. With help from the Trust for Public Land, PECO, and others, the school is replacing pavement with a landscaped yard open to the neighborhood. Students who got free bikes last year will have a place to ride. It's not just a playground—it's an investment in the community's future.

stories.title: Local Author Visits Every Corner Of South Dakota’s Communities
stories.summary: Author Seth Varner has visited every incorporated community in South Dakota, from Sioux Falls to the tiniest prairie towns. His book 'Wandermore in South Dakota' documents hidden gems, places to eat, and local secrets. He's now on a mission to explore all 50 states, one town at a time.
stories.extended_summary: What started as a COVID-era disruption turned into a full-blown exploration project for Seth Varner. He's already visited six states, with South Dakota leaving such a mark that he chose Deadwood for his bachelor party. Varner's journey isn't just about checking places off a list — he soaks in each community's unique character, sharing finds that don't make travel brochures. His social media gives followers a front-row seat to discovering America's heartland.

stories.summary: Fort Kent Cinema is putting local filmmaker Oliver Caron on the big screen this month, showing his work from the Maine Mayhem Film Festival. Caron, now a college student, wrote, directed, and acted in several shorts including a mockumentary about a man addicted to eating chicken in the shower. It's a win for hometown talent and community cinema.
stories.extended_summary: Oliver Caron's films — from a 'Spinal Tap'-style mockumentary called 'Shower Chicken' to a short titled 'The Loon' — are getting a hometown screening at Fort Kent Cinema. The theater, which previously live-streamed local basketball victories, is going beyond mainstream releases to spotlight regional creators. For Caron, it means his family doesn't have to drive six hours to see his work. The event introduces northern Maine audiences to the Portland festival's quirky, creative energy.

stories.title: Not lost in translation: training AI to speak African languages
stories.summary: A team from the University of Cape Town has built MzansiLM, a language model that includes all 11 of South Africa's official written languages. While big tech AI ignores over 98% of African languages, this small model aims to close the gap. It's a step toward making AI accessible to millions who are currently left out of the digital revolution.
stories.extended_summary: With over 2,000 languages in Africa but only 41 supported by major AI systems, the digital divide is massive. Researchers Anri Lombard, Jan Buys, Francois Meyer, and their team created MzansiLM to represent South Africa's linguistic diversity. Alongside it, they released MzansiText, the dataset used for training. Despite being small compared to corporate LLMs, MzansiLM showed strong performance in tests, proving that targeted, inclusive AI can punch above its weight.
stories.fake_headline: High School Students Create App That Reduces Food Waste in Cafeterias

stories.title: Pride Kel-So Returns
stories.summary: Pride Kel-So is returning for a second year after drawing over 1,600 people to the Keller-Southlake area, despite a hostile political climate. Organizers April and Shaina Dreyson say last year's event was healing for many in this conservative stronghold. This year's celebration is set for October 3 at the same church location.
stories.extended_summary: Despite threats and fear from local politicians' rhetoric, Pride Kel-So drew a crowd that proved the need for LGBTQ+ visibility in the Keller-Southlake area. Over 3,000 registered, though many stayed away due to safety concerns. Organizers are determined to keep the momentum going, creating a space where community members can find joy and acceptance. The second annual event promises more music, performances, and a chance to heal — on their own terms.
stories.fake_headline: Community Garden Grows 500 Pounds of Vegetables for Local Food Bank

stories.title: Rescuing London’s Precious Building Materials Diverting Them from Dumps for Reuse
stories.summary: Yes Make is rescuing London's architectural treasures — wood, brick, glass, and steel — from landfills and turning them into new objects with cultural potential. Operating from a five-acre salvage yard called Tipping Point East, the project creates a circular economy for the city's building materials. Even a 105-year-old sequoia tree found a second life through their workshops.
stories.extended_summary: Frustrated by red tape and waste, Joel De Mowbray founded Yes Make to salvage London's historic materials before they hit the dump. Partnering with Material Cultures, they secured Tipping Point East — now the UK's largest salvage site. More than half of the UK's waste comes from construction, and Yes Make is proving that beautiful, useful objects can emerge from what others discard. From a 105-year-old sequoia to Victorian bricks, every piece gets a chance to tell a new story.
fake_endings: Creating a Circular Economy for Construction
stories.fake_headline: New Recycling Program Turns Plastic Waste Into School Furniture

stories.title: Success: Balloon team that launched from Maine makes history
stories.summary: The Atlantic Explorer hydrogen balloon made history by landing safely in Luxembourg, completing the first trans-Atlantic crossing by an open-basket hydrogen balloon in nearly 20 years. Pilot Alicia Hempleman-Adams became the first woman to cross the Atlantic in a gas balloon. The three-person team launched from Presque Isle, Maine, and touched down after an epic journey.
stories.extended_summary: After a tense flight from Presque Isle, the Atlantic Explorer touched down near Diekirch, Luxembourg, early Sunday morning. The three pilots — Bert Padelt, Alicia Hempleman-Adams, and Peter Cuneo — are the first to cross the Atlantic in an open-basket hydrogen balloon. For Hempleman-Adams, it also made history as the first woman to achieve the feat, joining her father as one half of the only father-daughter duo to each cross by balloon. Tired but elated, the team is already dreaming of beds and showers.
stories.headline_split: Success: Balloon team that launched from Maine
stories.fake_headline: Volunteers Build Wheelchair Ramps for 50 Homes in One Weekend

stories.title: Vergas native opens Lakes Country Healthcare in DL
stories.summary: Physician assistant Ashley Schultz opened Lakes Country Healthcare in Detroit Lakes, a cash-pay clinic designed to slash costs and cut through red tape. The model gained popularity after COVID, and Schultz hopes to make healthcare accessible for families like hers, where high deductibles kept her from seeking care for her own kids. It's a fresh approach to rural medicine.
stories.extended_summary: After years of seeing patients struggle with high deductibles — even as a healthcare worker herself — Ashley Schultz decided to build a better option. Lakes Country Healthcare operates on a cash-pay model with transparent pricing, no surprise bills, and no insurance bureaucracy. Schultz, a mother of four, knows firsthand how cost barriers can delay care: her daughter once walked on a broken ankle for two days. Her clinic aims to change that for the Detroit Lakes community, one affordable visit at a time.
fake_endings: a new clinic for rural residents
a telemedicine service across the county
stories.fake_headline: Teacher Uses 3D Printer to Create Prosthetic Hands for Students

stories.title: ‘What’s more I-O-W-A, Americana, “76 Trombones” than a marching band?’: Iowa Pride Ensembles are making noise all June
stories.summary: What started as a Google spreadsheet and pizza meetings turned into Iowa Pride Ensembles, the state's first LGBTQIA+ marching, concert, and jazz bands. They're performing at Pride events across Iowa this June, including Capital City Pride Fest in Des Moines. Co-founders Rachel Gulick and Amanda Thomas-Murphy built a joyful musical movement for visibility and belonging.
stories.extended_summary: After seeing Pride bands in other states, Rachel Gulick created a spreadsheet the day after Thanksgiving 2024 and started recruiting musicians. Within months, Iowa Pride Ensembles became the state's only Pride band affiliated with the National Pride Bands Alliance. Their marching band is already booked for multiple Pride festivals, and the concert band starts in the fall. Co-founders say it's about more than music — it's a joyful resistance and a way to show that Iowa's LGBTQ+ community is here to stay, playing '76 Trombones' loud and proud.
stories.headline_split: ‘What’s more I-O-W-A, Americana, “76 Trombones” than a marching band?’: Iowa Pride Ensembles are making
stories.fake_headline: Local Mom Organizes Free Summer Science Camp for Underprivileged Kids
